summary: Nadia reflects on leaving Collaborative Fund after one year, framing her departure through Milan Kundera's concept of 'Es muss sein' - the idea that our most meaningful choices feel like inescapable fate rather than free will. She joined to work on experimental alternatives to traditional VC but shifted to deploying their seed fund. Despite loving the organization and its values, she couldn't ignore what was 'screaming most loudly' in her head. She expresses gratitude for what venture capital taught her about human nature while signaling a move toward new ways of directing capital to ideas that power the internet.
key_points: Meaningful life choices feel less like free will and more like inescapable imperatives we cannot ignore
She pivoted from her original experimental role to supporting core fund deployment out of loyalty to the organization
Despite perfect alignment on paper, she couldn't suppress what felt like her true calling
Venture capital revealed both 'the weird and wonderful nature of the human condition' and human capacity for kindness
She's moving toward finding new ways to fund valuable internet-related ideas
money_quotes: We do not make them out of free will; our choices are an inescapable fate, an 'imperative enslaving us'.
We can't ignore the things that scream most loudly in our heads, even when our paper lives seem to check all the boxes.
Venture capital showed me the weird and wonderful nature of the human condition.
It also gave me deep faith in the capacity of my fellow humans for kindness and generosity.
Finding new ways to direct capital towards valuable ideas that power the internet.
key_insight: The choices that define us aren't made through rational deliberation but through an inner imperative we eventually cannot resist, even when everything looks right on paper.
